perturbation.original_text: This Agreement represents the entire underst anding between WECC and Data Recipient related to the Non-Public Information and supersed es all previous communications between WECC and Data Recipient related to this subject. Th is Agreement can only be modified by written agreement executed by WECC and Data Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement represents the entire understanding between WECC and Data Recipient related to the Non-Public Information and supersedes all previous communications between WECC and Data Recipient related to this subject.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause that states the agreement can only be modified by written agreement executed by both parties, we are creating a situation where WECC might attempt to unilaterally modify the agreement. This conflicts with the principle of mutual consent and good faith dealings required in contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-209. Modification, Rescission and Waiver.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section of the UCC requires mutual agreement for contract modifications. Removing the clause requiring written agreement by both parties could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent this requirement, allowing for unilateral changes which are not legally sound.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-209. Modification, Rescission and Waiver. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-209. Modification, Rescission and Waiver. (1) An agreement modifying a contract within this Article needs no consideration to be binding. (2) A signed agreement which excludes modification or rescission except by a signed writing cannot be otherwise modified or rescinded, but except as between merchants such a requirement on a form supplied by the merchant must be separately signed by the other party. (3) The requirements of the statute of frauds section of this Article (Section 2-201 ) must be satisfied if the contract as modified is within its provisions. (4)Although an attempt at modification or rescission does not satisfy the requirements of subsection (2) or (3) it can operate as a waiver. (5) A party who has made a waiver affecting an executory portion of the contract may retract the waiver by reasonable notification received by the other party that strict performance will be required of any term waived, unless the retraction would be unjust in view of a material change of position in reliance on the waiver. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes

perturbation.justification: The original contract requires modifications to be made via written agreement executed by both parties. The changed text removes this requirement. Scraped Law Snippet 1, UCC § 2-209, states that a signed agreement which excludes modification except by a signed writing cannot be otherwise modified. By removing the requirement for a written agreement, the modified contract contradicts this legal principle, as it opens the door for modifications without mutual written consent, which the UCC aims to prevent. This creates a contradiction as the original agreement ensures adherence to legally sound modification processes, while the changed text potentially allows for unilateral changes.

perturbation.original_text: Upon any termination of this Agreement, Data Recipient shall return to WECC all Non-Public Information in Data Recipient’s possession or destroy all Non-Public Information in Data Recipient’s possession and certify to WECC in writ ing that all Non-Public Information has been returned or destroyed, except as may be ot herwise required by law in which case the confidentiality obligations of this Ag reement shall survive termination.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon any termination of this Agreement, Data Recipient shall return to WECC all Non-Public Information in Data Recipient’s possession or destroy all Non-Public Information in Data Recipient’s possession.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase about legal requirements overriding the return/destruction of data and the survival of confidentiality obligations, the contract now implies that all data *must* be returned or destroyed regardless of any legal holds, subpoenas, or other legal obligations to preserve the data. This can violate legal discovery rules.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 37(e) deals with the failure to preserve electronically stored information in litigation. The original clause acknowledges that legal obligations might supersede the contract's destruction requirement, which is vital for compliance with discovery rules. By removing the caveat, the contract directly contradicts the obligation to preserve evidence, potentially leading to sanctions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 37. Failure to Make Disclosures or to Cooperate in Discovery; Sanctions | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 37. Failure to Make Disclosures or to Cooperate in Discovery; Sanctions (a) Motion for an Order Compelling Disclosure or Discovery. (1) In General. On notice to other parties and all affected persons, a party may move for an order compelling disclosure or discovery. The motion must include a certification that the movant has in good faith conferred or attempted to confer with the person or party failing to make disclosure or discovery in an effort to obtain it without court action. (2) Appropriate Court. A motion for an order to a party must be made in the court where the action is pending. A motion for an order to a nonparty must be made in the court where the discovery is or will be taken. (3) Specific Motions. (A) To Compel Disclosure. If a party fails to make a disclosure required by Rule 26(a) , any other party may move to compel disclosure and for appropriate sanctions. (B) To Compel a Discovery Response. A party seeking discovery may move for an order compelling an answer, designation, production, or inspection. This motion may be made if: (i) a deponent fails to answer a question asked under Rule 30 or 31 ; (ii) a corporation or other entity fails to make a designation under Rule 30(b)(6) or 31(a)(4) ; (iii) a party fails to answer an interrogatory submitted under Rule 33 ; or (iv) a party fails to produce documents or fails to respond that inspection will be permitted—or fails to permit inspection—as requested under Rule 34 . (C) Related to a Deposition. When taking an oral deposition, the party asking a question may complete or adjourn the examination before moving for an order. (4) Evasive or Incomplete Disclosure, Answer, or Response. For purposes of this subdivision (a), an evasive or incomplete disclosure, answer, or response must be treated as a failur

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the exception that allows the Data Recipient to retain Non-Public Information if required by law, such as during legal proceedings where data preservation is necessary. The original text acknowledges that legal obligations may supersede the contract's destruction requirement. By removing this caveat, the contract directly contradicts the obligation to preserve evidence potentially leading to sanctions under FRCP 37. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is general, Scraped Law Snippet 1 directly references Rule 37, which deals with failure to preserve electronically stored information, reinforcing the importance of the original clause.

perturbation.original_text: Data Recipient agrees to comply with all applicable United States export laws and regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: Data Recipient agrees to comply with all regulations.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit reference to 'United States export laws' weakens the clause and creates ambiguity. This omission could lead to a violation of U.S. export control laws if the Data Recipient is outside the U.S. or if the data is shared internationally, as it removes a clear directive to follow specific, critical regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Export Administration Regulations (EAR) govern the export and re-export of sensitive goods, software, and technology. By removing the explicit reference to U.S. export laws, the altered clause becomes vague and unenforceable. It does not clearly instruct the data recipient to adhere to U.S. EAR, creating a risk of non-compliance if the data is exported from the U.S. or shared with foreign nationals, even inadvertently.

Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.

perturbation.justification: The changed text, by omitting "United States export laws and regulations," creates a legal contradiction. The original text specifically mandates compliance with U.S. export laws, while the altered text only requires compliance with general "regulations." This weakens the obligation and introduces ambiguity regarding adherence to U.S. export control laws. Snippet 1, referring to the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774, emphasizes the importance of these specific regulations. Snippet 2, mentioning Subchapter C Export Administration Regulations, further highlights the significance of these regulations. The omission undermines the clear directive to follow specific U.S. export regulations, creating a risk of non-compliance, thus forming a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Neither WECC nor any owner or submitter of the Non-Public Information shall be liable for any damages arising out of the use of the Non-Public Information.
perturbation.changed_text: WECC is not liable for any damages arising out of the use of the Non-Public Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'nor any owner or submitter of the Non-Public Information', the modified agreement implies that only WECC is shielded from liability, potentially exposing the owners or submitters of the information to liability claims. This creates a legal contradiction because owners and submitters may have rights to protection from liability under various state laws, especially if they acted in good faith.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 47 provides a privilege against liability for certain publications or broadcasts made without malice. By removing the liability shield for 'any owner or submitter', the contract potentially opens them up to defamation claims or other liabilities related to the information they submitted, which could be protected under California law. This creates a contradiction because the contract now provides less protection than the law might otherwise allow.

PERSONS [38 - 86] ( Heading of Division 1 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 12. ) PART 2. PERSONAL RIGHTS [43 - 53.7] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) 47. A privileged publication or broadcast is one made: (a) In the proper discharge of an official duty. (b) In any (1) legislative proceeding, (2) judicial proceeding, (3) in any other official proceeding authorized by law, or (4) in the initiation or course of any other proceeding authorized by law and reviewable pursuant to Chapter 2 (commencing with Section 1084) of Title 1 of Part 3 of the Code of Civil Procedure, except as follows: (1) An allegation or averment contained in any pleading or affidavit filed in an action for marital dissolution or legal separation made of or concerning a person by or against whom no affirmative relief is prayed in the action shall not be a privileged publication or broadcast as to the person making the allegation or averment within the meaning of this section unless the pleading is verified or affidavit sworn to, and is made without malice, by one having reasonable and probable cause for believing the truth of the allegation or averment and unless the allegation or averment is material and relevant to the issues in the action. (2) This subdivision does not make privileg
perturbation.justification: The modification removes liability protection for 'any owner or submitter of the Non-Public Information,' potentially exposing them to claims they would have been shielded from under the original agreement. The Law Explanation points to California Civil Code § 47, which provides a privilege against liability for certain publications. While Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing CIV 47, focuses on privileged publications or broadcasts, the core principle it establishes is the existence of legally protected privileges against liability. By removing the liability shield for owners and submitters, the modified contract potentially contradicts this protection, as they might now be exposed to claims related to the information they submitted. This creates a situation where the contract provides less protection than the law might allow, fitting the definition of an Omission Legal Contradiction by removing essential protections.
